title: HLSL Deep Dive
primer:
HLSL has evolved far beyond its DirectX 9 roots. Modern HLSL — compiled by DXC (DirectX Shader Compiler) and targeting Shader Model 6 — introduces features that have no direct counterpart in GLSL ES: constant buffers with explicit register binding, root constants, typed resource views, and Wave (subgroup) intrinsics.

Key HLSL-specific concepts you will work with in this lesson:

  • cbuffer — a constant buffer block, similar to a GLSL uniform block. Bound to a specific register slot with : register(b0).
  • Register binding — HLSL resources (buffers, textures, samplers) are bound by name to register slots: b for constant buffers, t for shader resource views, u for unordered-access views, s for samplers.
  • Shader Model 6 (SM6) — enables DXIL bytecode and unlocks Wave intrinsics, mesh/amplification shaders, and DXR ray tracing.
  • Wave intrinsics — SM6 functions like WaveActiveSum(), WaveGetLaneIndex(), and WavePrefixSum() allow threads in a wave (warp) to communicate without shared memory. The GLSL equivalent is the GL_KHR_shader_subgroup extension.

Each challenge asks you to port a piece of idiomatic HLSL to valid GLSL ES 3.0 — understanding why the translation is what it is, not just what to type.

challenges.title: Port a cbuffer to GLSL uniform block
hints: The cbuffer members become members of the GLSL uniform block — access them by the same bare names (baseColor, roughness, metallic, tiling).
std140 layout requires explicit padding to align floats — the _pad members in the template handle that. Just fill in the logic.
challenges.unlocks_theory: HLSL's cbuffer maps to GLSL's named uniform block (UBO). The key difference is layout packing: HLSL packs cbuffer members tightly by default; GLSL's std140 layout has strict alignment rules (vec3 pads to vec4 size, arrays align to vec4 boundaries). WebGL2 does not support the binding=N qualifier on uniform blocks — block binding must be set at the API level with gl.uniformBlockBinding(). DXC's /Zpc flag controls HLSL column/row-major order. For cross-API work, prefer std430 layout on compute SSBOs and std140 on uniform blocks.

hlsl.note: Original HLSL cbuffer version — the source for this porting exercise.

slang.note: Slang can use ParameterBlock or a plain uniform cbuffer; here shown with ParameterBlock for modern style.

challenges.title: Texture sampling: Texture2D + SamplerState
hints: GLSL combines texture + sampler into sampler2D. Use texture(u_albedo, uv) instead of gAlbedo.Sample(gSampler, uv).
The animated UV offset just needs vec2() instead of float2() — the math is identical. Use v_uv from the quad vertex shader.
challenges.unlocks_theory: HLSL separates textures (Texture2D, Texture3D, TextureCube) from samplers (SamplerState, SamplerComparisonState) to allow one texture to be sampled with different filtering modes. GLSL combines them into opaque sampler types (sampler2D, sampler3D, samplerCube). Slang supports both models and can cross-compile either way. DirectX 12 and Vulkan both expose the separate model natively; the combined model is an OpenGL/WebGL convention.

hlsl.note: Original HLSL with separate Texture2D and SamplerState.

slang.note: Slang uses HLSL-style Texture2D.Sample(); when targeting GLSL, the compiler generates combined sampler2D code.

challenges.title: Wave intrinsics → visualize subgroup lanes in a fragment shader
challenges.description:
Shader Model 6 Wave intrinsics let threads in a wave communicate without shared memory. Key intrinsics: `WaveGetLaneIndex()` (this thread's index within the wave) and `WaveGetLaneCount()` (wave size). The GLSL equivalents use the GL_KHR_shader_subgroup extension: `gl_SubgroupInvocationID` and `gl_SubgroupSize`.

In a fragment shader, you cannot use subgroup extensions directly in WebGL2 — but you can simulate the lane-coloring concept using the pixel's UV position as a proxy for its 'lane index'.

HLSL Wave intrinsic reference:
```hlsl
// In a wave of 32 lanes, color each lane differently
uint laneIdx   = WaveGetLaneIndex();   // 0..31
uint laneCount = WaveGetLaneCount();   // e.g. 32
float t = float(laneIdx) / float(laneCount);
```

Write a GLSL ES 3.0 fragment shader that:
- Simulates a 32-lane wave using `v_uv.x` as the lane index proxy
- Computes `float laneT = fract(v_uv.x * 32.0) / 1.0` — normalized position within a 'lane'
- Colors the output with a hue cycle: use `laneT` to rotate through red→green→blue using sin with phase offsets
- Adds a vertical dividing line every 1/32 of width (every lane boundary)
hints: floor(v_uv.x * 32.0) gives you an integer lane index 0..31. Divide by 32.0 to get a normalized hue value.
sin(hue * 6.2832 + offset) with offsets 0, 2.094 (2π/3), and 4.189 (4π/3) produces the red, green, and blue channels of a full hue cycle.
challenges.unlocks_theory: SM6 Wave intrinsics and GLSL subgroup operations are the same hardware feature — SIMD lane communication — under different names. The mapping is consistent: WaveActiveSum→subgroupAdd, WaveActiveBitOr→subgroupOr, WavePrefixSum→subgroupExclusiveAdd, WaveIsFirstLane()→subgroupElect(), WaveGetLaneIndex()→gl_SubgroupInvocationID, WaveGetLaneCount()→gl_SubgroupSize. Vulkan exposes these via VK_EXT_shader_subgroup_basic. WebGL2 does not expose subgroup extensions at all — the closest you can get is simulating lane-like concepts using UV coordinates as shown here. When writing cross-platform GPU compute, prefer Slang's generic wave intrinsics which compile to both targets.

hlsl.note: HLSL SM6 compute shader showing true Wave intrinsics for lane visualization.

slang.note: Slang abstracts wave/subgroup ops — the same source compiles to both DXIL and SPIR-V.
